content.text:
Rings appear throughout mathematics. Understanding key examples is 
essential for developing intuition about ring theory.

content.title: Classical number rings
content.problem:
- $\Z$ (integers) — the fundamental ring of arithmetic

- $\Q, \R, \C$ — fields (rings where every nonzero element has 
 a multiplicative inverse)

- $\Z[i] = \{a + bi : a, b \in \Z\}$ — the Gaussian integers

- $\Z[\sqrt{2}] = \{a + b\sqrt{2} : a, b \in \Z\}$
All are commutative rings with unity.

content.title: $\Z_n$ as a ring
content.problem:
$\Z_n$ with addition and multiplication modulo $n$ is a commutative 
ring with unity $1$.

For $n = 6$: $2 \cdot 3 = 6 \equiv 0 \pmod{6}$.

Note: $\Z_n$ has zero divisors when $n$ is composite.

content.title: Zero Divisor
content.content:
A **zero divisor** in a ring $R$ is a nonzero 
element $a$ such that $ab = 0$ for some nonzero $b \in R$.

content.title: Polynomials
content.problem:
If $R$ is a ring, then $R[x]$ (polynomials in $x$ with coefficients 
from $R$) is also a ring under usual polynomial addition and 
multiplication.

- $\Z[x]$ — integer polynomials

- $\R[x]$ — real polynomials

- $\Z_p[x]$ — polynomials over $\Z_p$

content.title: Matrices
content.problem:
For any ring $R$, the set $M_n(R)$ of $n \times n$ matrices with entries 
from $R$ forms a ring under matrix addition and multiplication.

Important: $M_n(R)$ is **not commutative** for $n \geq 2$, even 
if $R$ is commutative.

content.text: The Trivial Ring and Boolean Ring

content.title: Special rings
content.problem:
- The **trivial ring** $\{0\}$ has $0 = 1$. (This is the 
 only ring where $0 = 1$.)

- The **Boolean ring** $\{0, 1\}$ with $1 + 1 = 0$ (XOR for 
 addition, AND for multiplication).

content.title: Subring
content.content:
A subset $S$ of a ring $R$ is a **subring** if $S$ 
is itself a ring under the operations of $R$.

content.title: Subring Test
content.content:
A nonempty subset $S \subseteq R$ is a subring if and only if:

1. $a - b \in S$ for all $a, b \in S$

2. $ab \in S$ for all $a, b \in S$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Subrings
content.problem:
- $\Z$ is a subring of $\Q$ which is a subring of $\R$ which is 
 a subring of $\C$.

- $2\Z$ (even integers) is a subring of $\Z$, but has no unity.

- $\{a + bi : a, b \in \Z\}$ is a subring of $\C$.

items:
Verify that $\Z[\sqrt{5}] = \{a + b\sqrt{5} : a, b \in \Z\}$ is 
 a ring.

Show that $M_2(\Z_2)$ has $16$ elements. How many are zero 
 divisors?

Prove that $\{a + bi : a \equiv b \pmod{2}\}$ is a subring of 
 $\Z[i]$.

Is the set $\{f : \R \to \R\}$ of all functions a ring under 
 pointwise operations?

marginNotes.text:
Great is our Lord and mighty in power; 
his understanding has no limit.
